title: Dog Soft Fatty Lump on Ribcage: Causes and Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: Found a soft, fatty lump on your dog's ribs? Learn about lipomas, urgency levels, and how photos help your vet assess the growth.
content_html:
TL;DR: A soft, moveable lump on your dog's ribcage is likely a benign fatty lipoma and rarely an emergency, though you should schedule a routine vet visit to confirm. If the growth is rapid or painful, seek care sooner.

What should I know about a soft lump on my dog's ribcage?

Finding a new growth on your pet can be worrying, but a soft, moveable lump on the ribcage is very common in dogs, especially as they age. Most often, these are lipomas, which are benign (non-cancerous) tumors made of fat cells. They typically feel squishy, like a small balloon or a packet of butter under the skin, and they usually do not cause the dog any pain or discomfort.

Is a soft lump on my dog's ribs a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for a soft, fatty lump on the ribcage is generally Low. While any new growth should be evaluated by a veterinarian to confirm it is not something more serious, it is rarely an emergency. You should schedule a visit sooner if the lump:

  • Is growing rapidly
  • Appears red or inflamed
  • Seems to be causing your dog pain

Why should I take a photo of the lump on my dog?

  • Capture a clear photo of the lump to serve as a triage tool for your veterinary team.
  • Use a common object like a coin or a ruler next to the lump to provide a sense of scale.
  • Track whether the lump is changing in size, shape, or color over time through photos.
  • Provide these images to your vet to help determine if the pet needs to be seen urgently or monitored at home.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Lipomas, soft and occasionally pedunculated nodular masses, are common in older, obese female dogs, particularly Doberman Pinschers, Labrador Retrievers, Miniature Schnauzers, and mixed-breed dogs, typically on the trunk and proximal limbs. While benign, lipomas tend to enlarge and can be indistinguishable from infiltrative lipomas or liposarcomas. Infiltrative lipomas, rare in dogs, present as poorly confined, soft, nodular to diffuse swellings on the thorax and limbs, involving subcutaneous fat and underlying muscle; these are considered sarcomas of intermediate malignancy. Liposarcomas, rare in all domestic animals, usually develop on the trunk and extremities of older male dogs, with Shetland Sheepdogs and Beagles potentially predisposed.

Chapter: Oncology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 958)

faqs.question: Can these fatty lumps turn into cancer?
faqs.answer: True lipomas are benign and do not spread to other parts of the body, but they can occasionally grow large enough to interfere with your dog's movement.

faqs.question: How will my vet diagnose the lump?
faqs.answer: Most veterinarians will perform a Fine Needle Aspirate (FNA) by using a small needle to collect cells from the lump and examine them under a microscope to confirm it is fat.

faqs.question: Does my dog need surgery to remove it?
faqs.answer: Surgery is usually not required for fatty lumps unless they are growing very quickly, causing pain, or located in an area that restricts the dog's mobility, such as the armpit.
